hero.tagline: Nevada's small claims courts offer a streamlined process for disputes up to $10,000, with hearings typically scheduled within 30 to 60 days.

at_a_glance.label: Most you can sue for
at_a_glance.detail: Same cap for individuals and businesses

at_a_glance.label: Appeal window
at_a_glance.detail: Trial de novo in District Court

at_a_glance.label: Recent change
at_a_glance.detail: No major changes in the last 3 years

limits.split_claims_explanation: If your claim exceeds $10,000, you cannot split it into multiple claims to fit within the small claims limit. Instead, you must file in a higher court.

claim_categories.title: Contracts and money owed
claim_categories.blurb: Nevada allows you to file claims for breaches of contract and money owed up to $10,000. The statute of limitations varies: 6 years for written contracts and 4 years for oral contracts.

claims.name: Breach of a written contract
claims.example: A contractor signed a $4,000 contract to remodel your kitchen, took the deposit, and never finished.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Bring the signed contract, payment proof, and any correspondence showing the breach.

claims.name: Breach of an oral contract
claims.example: You agreed verbally to lend a friend $2,000, which they promised to repay in 6 months, but they haven't.
claims.notes: 4-year deadline from breach. Gather any evidence of the agreement, such as emails or witness statements.

claims.name: Unpaid personal loan
claims.example: You lent $5,000 to a colleague with the understanding they'd repay in a year, but they haven't.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline if documented in writing; 4 years if oral. Provide any written agreement or proof of the loan.

claims.name: Default on a promissory note
claims.example: A borrower signed a promissory note to repay $8,000 over 12 months but stopped after 3 payments.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from default. Present the original promissory note and records of payments made.

claims.name: Unpaid invoice for services rendered
claims.example: You provided graphic design services worth $3,500, invoiced the client, but haven't received payment.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from when payment was due. Include the invoice, service agreement, and any communication regarding payment.

claims.name: Unpaid balance on an open account
claims.example: A customer has an outstanding balance of $2,000 on their store credit account and refuses to pay.
claims.notes: 4-year deadline from the last charge or payment. Provide account statements and records of transactions.

claims.name: Unpaid rent by tenant
claims.example: Your tenant missed 3 months of rent totaling $4,500 and has since vacated the property.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from when rent was due. Bring the lease agreement, payment records, and any notices sent.

claims.name: Payment with a bad check
claims.example: A customer paid for $1,200 worth of goods with a check that bounced due to insufficient funds.
claims.notes: After a 30-day written notice, you may recover the check amount plus damages. Provide the bounced check and notice sent.
claims.damage_boost: Check amount + service charge up to $25 + civil penalty up to $500 OR 3x check amount, whichever is greater.

claims.name: Co-signer held liable for default
claims.example: You co-signed a $6,000 loan for a friend who defaulted, and now the lender is seeking payment from you.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from default. Present the loan agreement and any correspondence from the lender.

claim_categories.title: Consumer disputes
claim_categories.blurb: Nevada's small claims courts handle various consumer disputes up to $10,000, including issues with products, services, and false advertising.

claims.name: Purchase of a defective product
claims.example: You bought a $900 appliance that stopped working after a week, and the seller refuses to refund or replace it.
claims.notes: 4-year deadline from purchase. Bring the receipt, warranty, and any communication with the seller.

claims.name: Substandard service provided
claims.example: A cleaning service charged $500 but left your home in poor condition, and they refuse to redo the work.
claims.notes: 4-year deadline from service date. Provide the service agreement, photos of the work, and correspondence.

claims.name: Unsatisfactory auto repair
claims.example: A mechanic charged $1,200 for repairs that didn't fix the issue, and they refuse to refund or correct the work.
claims.notes: 4-year deadline from repair date. Bring the repair invoice, any warranties, and records of subsequent issues.

claims.name: Contractor failed to complete work
claims.example: You paid a contractor $7,500 to renovate your bathroom, but they abandoned the project halfway through.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Provide the contract, payment records, and evidence of incomplete work.

claims.name: Vendor failed to deliver services
claims.example: You hired a DJ for $1,000 for your wedding, but they never showed up and won't refund your payment.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Bring the service agreement, payment proof, and any communication.

claims.name: Unauthorized charges after subscription cancellation
claims.example: You canceled a $50/month gym membership, but they continued to charge your card for 3 months.
claims.notes: 4-year deadline from unauthorized charge. Provide cancellation confirmation and bank statements.

claims.name: Purchase of a defective used car
claims.example: You bought a used car for $9,000 that broke down within a week, and the dealer refuses to address the issue.
claims.notes: 4-year deadline from purchase. Bring the sales contract, any warranties, and repair records.

claims.name: False advertising leading to financial loss
claims.example: A store advertised a 'buy one, get one free' deal, but charged you full price for both items totaling $200.
claims.notes: 4-year deadline from purchase. Provide the advertisement and receipt.

claim_categories.title: Property damage
claim_categories.blurb: Nevada's small claims courts address property damage claims up to $10,000, including vehicle collisions and damage to personal property.

claims.name: Damage from a vehicle collision
claims.example: Another driver hit your parked car, causing $3,000 in damages, and refuses to pay for repairs.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from accident date. Bring the police report, repair estimates, and photos.

claims.name: Neighbor caused damage to your property
claims.example: Your neighbor's tree fell onto your fence, causing $2,500 in damages, and they won't cover the cost.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from damage date. Provide photos, repair estimates, and any communication with the neighbor.

claims.name: Tenant caused damage to rental property
claims.example: A tenant left your rental unit with $4,000 in damages beyond normal wear and tear.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from discovery of damage. Bring the lease agreement, move-in/move-out inspection reports, and repair invoices.

claims.name: Damage caused by someone's pet
claims.example: A neighbor's dog entered your yard and destroyed $1,200 worth of landscaping.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from damage date. Provide photos, repair estimates, and any communication with the pet owner.

claims.name: Damage to property left in someone's care
claims.example: You left your laptop with a repair shop, and they returned it with a cracked screen, refusing to take responsibility.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from damage date. Bring the service agreement, photos of the damage, and any correspondence.

claim_categories.title: Landlord-tenant disputes
claim_categories.blurb: Nevada's small claims courts handle monetary disputes between landlords and tenants up to $10,000, excluding eviction cases.

claims.name: Failure to return security deposit
claims.example: Your landlord withheld your $1,500 security deposit without providing an itemized list of deductions.
claims.notes: 1-year deadline from lease termination. Landlords must provide an itemized list of deductions within 30 days.

claims.name: Security deposit deductions not itemized
claims.example: Your landlord deducted $800 from your deposit but didn't provide an itemized statement.
claims.notes: 1-year deadline from lease termination. Landlords are required to itemize deductions within 30 days.

claims.name: Rental unit deemed uninhabitable
claims.example: Your apartment had no heating during winter, and the landlord refused to make repairs.
claims.notes: 1-year deadline from discovery. Tenants may withhold rent or terminate the lease if the landlord fails to maintain habitable conditions.

claims.name: Landlord wrongfully retained tenant's property
claims.example: After eviction, your landlord kept your furniture worth $3,000 and refuses to return it.
claims.notes: 1-year deadline from property retention. Landlords must store abandoned property and provide notice before disposal.

claims.name: Landlord conducted an illegal eviction
claims.example: Your landlord changed the locks without notice, preventing you from accessing your apartment.
claims.notes: 1-year deadline from eviction. Self-help evictions are illegal; landlords must follow formal eviction procedures.

claims.name: Failure to return prepaid rent after lease termination
claims.example: You paid first and last month's rent, but after moving out, the landlord didn't return the last month's rent.
claims.notes: 1-year deadline from lease termination. Landlords must return prepaid rent within 30 days unless lawfully withheld.

claims.name: Tenant failed to pay rent
claims.example: Your tenant missed 2 months of rent totaling $2,800 and has since vacated the property.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from when rent was due. Provide the lease agreement and payment records.

claims.name: Tenant repaired and deducted from rent
claims.example: You paid $600 to fix a broken heater after the landlord ignored repair requests and deducted it from rent.
claims.notes: 1-year deadline from repair. Tenants must provide notice and allow the landlord 14 days to make repairs before deducting costs.

claim_categories.title: Employment and wage disputes
claim_categories.blurb: Nevada's small claims courts handle wage disputes up to $10,000, including unpaid wages and final paychecks.

claims.name: Employer failed to pay earned wages
claims.example: Your employer owes you $2,000 for hours worked but hasn't issued payment.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from when wages were due. Employers must pay all wages due at the end of each pay period.

claims.name: Employer failed to provide final paycheck
claims.example: After resigning, your employer didn't pay your final wages totaling $1,500.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from termination. Final wages are due immediately upon termination.

claims.name: Employer failed to pay earned commissions
claims.example: You earned $3,000 in sales commissions, but your employer refuses to pay.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from when commissions were due. Commissions are considered wages and must be paid accordingly.

claims.name: Employer failed to pay overtime wages
claims.example: You worked 10 hours of overtime but weren't compensated at the overtime rate.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from when overtime was due. Nevada law requires overtime pay for hours worked over 40 in a week.

claim_categories.title: Personal claims
claim_categories.blurb: Nevada's small claims courts address personal claims up to $10,000, including minor injuries and property disputes.

claims.name: Minor personal injury
claims.example: You slipped on a wet floor in a store, resulting in a $2,500 medical bill.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from injury date. Provide medical records and any incident reports.

claims.name: Failure to return loaned property
claims.example: You lent a friend your $1,200 camera, and they refuse to return it.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from demand for return. Provide evidence of the loan and any communication.

claims.name: Theft of personal property
claims.example: A neighbor took your $800 bicycle without permission and won't return it.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from theft date. Provide proof of ownership and any evidence of the theft.

claim_categories.title: Vehicle-specific disputes
claim_categories.blurb: Nevada's small claims courts handle vehicle-related disputes up to $10,000, including mechanic issues and wrongful towing.

claims.name: Dispute over mechanic's work
claims.example: A mechanic charged $2,000 for repairs that didn't fix the problem, and refuses to refund.
claims.notes: 4-year deadline from repair date. Provide the repair invoice and any communication.

claims.name: Vehicle wrongfully towed
claims.example: Your car was towed from your apartment complex without proper notice, resulting in $500 in fees.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from tow date. Provide towing receipts and any evidence of improper notice.

exclusions.explanation: Small claims courts in Nevada do not handle eviction cases; these must be filed in Justice Court under standard civil procedures.
Disputes involving the title to real property are beyond the jurisdiction of small claims courts.
Claims involving defamation, libel, or slander are not suitable for small claims court due to their complexity.
Professional malpractice claims require expert testimony and are not handled in small claims court.
Issues such as divorce, child custody, and support are outside the jurisdiction of small claims courts.
Disputes involving wills, estates, and trusts are not handled in small claims court.
Small claims courts do not have jurisdiction over cases against federal agencies.
Class action lawsuits are complex and exceed the procedural scope of small claims courts.
Small claims courts cannot issue injunctions or orders for specific performance.
Disputes over workers' compensation benefits are handled by the Nevada Department of Business and Industry.
exclusions.where_to_go_instead: Nevada Department of Business and Industry

exclusions.explanation: Bankruptcy cases are under federal jurisdiction and not handled in small claims court.
Disputes subject to mandatory arbitration clauses are not suitable for small claims court.
Claims against deceased individuals must be filed against their estate in probate court.

damages.punitive_explanation: Punitive damages are not awarded in small claims court; however, statutory penalties may apply in certain cases, such as bad check claims.

statutory_multipliers.explanation: Check amount + service charge up to $25 + civil penalty up to $500 OR 3x check amount, whichever is greater.

damages.attorney_fees_explanation: Attorney fees may be recoverable if specified by statute or contract; however, small claims court is designed for self-representation.

filing_options: Any county where one defendant resides
Where the cause of action arose

where_to_file.consequences_of_wrong_venue: Filing in the wrong venue may result in dismissal or transfer of the case, potentially causing delays and additional costs.
where_to_file.e_filing_notes: Availability of e-filing depends on the specific Justice Court; check with the local court for options.

forms.name: Application to Proceed In Forma Pauperis

eligibility_bullets: Receiving need-based public assistance (SNAP, TANF, SSI)
Household income below 125% federal poverty line
waiver.coverage_notes: The waiver covers filing fees and may cover service fees; check with the court for specifics.
waiver.if_you_win_notes: If you win, the waived fees may be added to the judgment against the loser.

fees.fees_recoverable_from_loser_notes: If you win, filing fee and reasonable service costs are added to the judgment.

entries.clock_starts: Date of last charge or payment

statute_of_limitations.discovery_rule_notes: The discovery rule applies to fraud claims, starting the statute of limitations when the fraud is discovered.
statute_of_limitations.tolling_notes: The statute of limitations may be tolled if the defendant is out of state, the plaintiff is a minor, or in cases of bankruptcy.

pre_filing.demand_letter_notes: While not required, sending a demand letter can often resolve disputes without court intervention and serves as evidence of your attempt to settle.
pre_filing.bad_check_notice_rule: For bad check claims, a 30-day written notice demanding payment is required before filing suit.
pre_filing.wage_claim_demand_rule: No specific pre-filing demand required for wage claims, but it's advisable to formally request payment before filing.
pre_filing.landlord_tenant_pre_filing_notes: Landlords must provide an itemized list of deductions from the security deposit within 30 days; failure to do so may entitle the tenant to the full deposit.
pre_filing.consumer_protection_notes: No specific pre-filing requirements for consumer protection claims, but documenting all communications is beneficial.

forms.name: Small Claims Affidavit of Complaint
forms.description: Initiates a small claims case by outlining the plaintiff's claim and the amount sought.

forms.name: Application to Proceed In Forma Pauperis
forms.description: Requests a waiver of court fees due to financial hardship.

forms.name: Notice of Appeal
forms.description: Filed to appeal a small claims judgment to the District Court.

methods.name: Sheriff personal service
methods.description: Sheriff or constable personally hands the papers to the defendant.

methods.name: Private process server
methods.description: A licensed individual delivers the papers to the defendant.

service.business_service_rule: Serve the registered agent; if none, serve an officer or managing agent.
service.out_of_state_defendant_notes: Service must comply with Nevada Rules of Civil Procedure and the laws of the state where service is made.
service.cant_find_defendant_notes: If the defendant cannot be located, you may request permission for alternative service methods, such as publication.
service.avoiding_service_notes: If the defendant is evading service, document your attempts and seek court approval for alternative service.

response_and_default.response_notes: Defendants are not required to file a written answer but must appear at the hearing; failure to appear may result in a default judgment.
response_and_default.default_process: If the defendant fails to appear, the court may grant a default judgment in favor of the plaintiff.
response_and_default.prove_up_notes: Even if the defendant defaults, the plaintiff must provide evidence to support the claim and amount sought.

motion_to_vacate_form.name: Motion to Set Aside Default Judgment

response_and_default.motion_to_vacate_appeal_notes: If the motion to vacate is denied, the defendant may appeal the denial within 5 business days.

counterclaim.transfer_to_higher_court_notes: If the counterclaim exceeds $10,000, the case may be transferred to District Court.

hearing.lawyers_allowed_notes: Parties may choose to be represented by an attorney, but it is not required.
hearing.format: Bench trial with relaxed rules of evidence; each party presents their case directly to the judge.
hearing.interpreter_request_process: Parties must arrange for their own interpreters; some courts may provide a list of certified interpreters.
hearing.decision_timing: On the spot or mailed within a few weeks
hearing.on_site_mediation_notes: Some courts offer mediation services to help parties reach a settlement before the hearing.

appeals.type_notes: The District Court conducts a new trial as if the small claims trial had not occurred.
appeals.default_judgment_notes: A default judgment cannot be appealed; the defendant must first file a motion to vacate the judgment.

collection.post_judgment_interest_notes: Interest accrues on the judgment amount from the date of entry until paid.

methods.name: Wage garnishment
methods.blurb: Have a portion of debtor's wages withheld until the judgment is paid.
methods.description: File a writ of garnishment with the court, serve the employer, and the employer withholds a portion of the debtor's wages to satisfy the judgment.
methods.effectiveness_notes: Most effective when debtor is W-2 employed at a stable job.

methods.name: Bank levy
methods.blurb: Seize funds from debtor's bank account to satisfy the judgment.
methods.description: Obtain a writ of execution, serve the bank, and the bank freezes and transfers funds to the court.
methods.effectiveness_notes: Effective if debtor maintains sufficient funds in bank accounts.

methods.name: Property lien
methods.blurb: Place a lien on debtor's real property, preventing sale or refinancing until judgment is paid.
methods.description: Record the judgment with the county recorder's office to create a lien on the debtor's property.
methods.effectiveness_notes: Effective if debtor owns real property; lien must be satisfied before property can be sold or refinanced.

methods.name: Writ of execution
methods.blurb: Authorize the sheriff to seize and sell debtor's non-exempt property to satisfy the judgment.
methods.description: Obtain a writ from the court, and the sheriff seizes and sells debtor's property at auction.
methods.effectiveness_notes: Effective if debtor owns valuable non-exempt personal property.

collection.judgment_renewal_process: File an affidavit of renewal with the court before the judgment expires to extend its enforceability.
collection.bankruptcy_effect_notes: Filing for bankruptcy may discharge the judgment; certain debts are non-dischargeable.
collection.priority_notes: Priority among multiple judgment creditors is generally determined by the order of recording or service.

businesses.self_representation_notes: Businesses may represent themselves in small claims court; however, some courts may require attorney representation for corporations or LLCs.
businesses.fictitious_name_notes: Businesses operating under a fictitious name (DBA) must ensure the name is properly registered.
businesses.sole_proprietor_employee_exception: Sole proprietors may represent themselves but cannot send an employee in their place.
businesses.licensing_notes: Unlicensed contractors may be barred from recovering fees for work requiring a license.

pitfalls.title: Exceeding the monetary limit
pitfalls.what_happens: If your claim exceeds $10,000, the case may be dismissed or you may be required to waive the excess amount.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Ensure your claim, including damages and costs, does not exceed the $10,000 limit for small claims court.

pitfalls.title: Improper service of process
pitfalls.what_happens: Failure to properly serve the defendant can result in delays or dismissal of your case.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Follow Nevada's rules for service of process carefully, using authorized methods and ensuring timely service.

pitfalls.title: Missing the statute of limitations
pitfalls.what_happens: Filing a claim after the statute of limitations has expired will lead to dismissal.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Be aware of the specific statute of limitations for your claim type and file within the allowed time frame.

pitfalls.title: Insufficient evidence
pitfalls.what_happens: Lack of evidence can result in losing your case, even if your claim is valid.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Gather all relevant documents, photos, and witness statements to support your claim.

pitfalls.title: Ignoring counterclaims
pitfalls.what_happens: If the defendant files a counterclaim and you are unprepared, you could lose more than you anticipated.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Be prepared to address potential counterclaims by understanding the defendant's possible arguments.

pitfalls.title: Failing to collect judgment
pitfalls.what_happens: Winning a judgment does not guarantee payment; you may need to take additional steps to collect.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Familiarize yourself with Nevada's collection methods and be prepared to pursue them if necessary.

faqs.question: Do I need a lawyer?
faqs.answer: No, you are not required to have a lawyer in Nevada's small claims court. The process is designed for individuals to represent themselves, though you may choose to hire an attorney if you prefer.

faqs.question: How long does it take?
faqs.answer: Small claims cases in Nevada are typically scheduled for a hearing within 30 to 60 days after filing, aiming for a prompt resolution.

faqs.question: What's the maximum I can sue for?
faqs.answer: The maximum amount you can sue for in Nevada's small claims court is $10,000. Claims exceeding this amount must be filed in a higher court.

faqs.question: What happens if the defendant doesn't show up?
faqs.answer: If the defendant fails to appear at the hearing, the court may grant a default judgment in your favor. However, you must still present evidence to support your claim.

faqs.question: Can I appeal?
faqs.answer: Yes, either party can appeal a small claims judgment to the District Court within 5 business days. The appeal is a trial de novo, meaning the case is heard anew.

faqs.question: How long is a judgment good for?
faqs.answer: In Nevada, a small claims judgment is valid for 6 years. You can renew the judgment before it expires to extend its enforceability.

state_specific_notes: Nevada's small claims courts are part of the Justice Court system, handling cases up to $10,000.
Eviction cases are not handled in small claims court and must be filed in Justice Court under standard civil procedures.
The statute of limitations varies by claim type; for example, 6 years for written contracts and 4 years for oral contracts.
Small claims judgments accrue interest at a rate of 5.25% per annum until paid.
Businesses may represent themselves in small claims court, but some courts may require attorney representation for corporations or LLCs.

interesting_findings: Nevada allows for statutory penalties in bad check cases, potentially awarding up to three times the check amount.
The state provides a self-help center with resources and forms for individuals pursuing small claims cases.
Some Justice Courts in Nevada offer mediation services to help parties resolve disputes before proceeding to a hearing.

sources.label: Nev. Rev. Stat. Chapter 11 (Statute of Limitations)
Nevada Rules of Civil Procedure Rule 4 (Service)
Nev. Rev. Stat. Chapter 21 (Execution and Supplementary Proceedings)
Nev. Rev. Stat. Chapter 598 (Deceptive Trade Practices)
Nev. Rev. Stat. § 41.620 (Bad Check Civil Action)
